_notes: District EdTech usage listing — 1:1 device programs, platforms, AI tools/policies, notable services, contracts, and student-data-privacy agreements. Compiled from district technology pages, handbooks, school-committee minutes, budget documents, local press, and the SDPC/Massachusetts Student Privacy Alliance registry (sdpc.a4l.org). A signed privacy agreement means a tool was vetted for use, not proof of active classroom deployment. Listing only — no rating or tiering.

publicCommentary.who: Lauren Harwood, Director of Instructional Technology
publicCommentary.what: 'AI is an incredibly powerful tool, and our philosophy is to use AI responsibly and educate our students on how to use it responsibly. Not just like, oh, this can write an essay for me.' The article notes the district 'employs vetted platforms like Magic School rather than allowing unrestricted access to consumer AI tools'

publicCommentary.who: Bill Runey, Superintendent
publicCommentary.what: 'AI is certainly top of mind. We don't just toss students the keys and hope they figure it out—we teach them how to drive cars responsibly. That same philosophy applies to staff.'

publicCommentary.who: Lauren Harwood, Director of Instructional Technology
publicCommentary.what: District 'has taken a stance against immediately blocking AI sites, choosing instead to focus on teaching students and staff how to use AI responsibly': 'Just because you ask ChatGPT a question, you need critical thinking skills when you look at the response. You still need to know how to find the answers.'

publicCommentary.who: Bill Runey, Superintendent
publicCommentary.what: On Harwood receiving Tech & Learning's New England Emerging Technology Leadership Award: 'She doesn't implement technology for the sake of innovation—she ensures it improves instruction, strengthens student outcomes, and prepares our students to think, create, and compete in a rapidly evolving world.' Article also credits district AI guidelines with increased teacher adoption of AI tools
